My Zones of Interest

My Zones

Observations

Hunting Terrain Glossary

The language of the land. Know these terms and you'll read terrain like a wildlife biologist.

Water & Movement Corridors

Where life funnels

Drainage

A natural channel where water flows downhill — can be a creek, river, or just a subtle low point.

Bears & elk LOVE these (food + water + travel). In summer: green, alive, shaded. Animals move through drainages like highways.

Creek / Stream / River

Moving water (size varies). Fish = bears (in some regions). Edges = lush vegetation = feeding zones.

Spring / Seep

Tiny, often hidden water source. Goldmine in dry areas — animals will travel specifically to these.

Bottom

Lowest part of a drainage or valley. Cooler temps, thicker vegetation. Often bedding nearby, feeding edges.

Terrain Shapes

Where animals travel + hide

Ridge

Long high spine of land. Animals use it to travel efficiently. Wind advantage — they scent below.

Spur Ridge

A smaller ridge branching off a main ridge. Classic ambush zone. Animals sidehill these constantly.

Saddle

Low point between two high points. Top-tier travel corridor.

Elk = highways. Deer = crossing points. If you're placing cams: this is a must-hit.

Knob

A rounded high point (like a mini peak). Great glassing spot. Animals may bed just below it for wind advantage.

Bench

Flat-ish shelf on a slope. Animals LOVE bedding here — especially elk and deer.

This is where you find them mid-day.

Bowl / Basin

Large, rounded depression. Holds feed + water. Often where animals stay if undisturbed.

Finger

Narrow strip of land extending from ridge downward. Animals move along these naturally. Connects ridge to drainage.

Vegetation & Cover

Where animals feel safe

Timber

Dense forested area. Bedding, shade, security. North-facing slopes = cooler = prime.

Open Face / Open Slope

Grass or sparse trees. Feeding zones, especially mornings and evenings.

Thick / Bedding Cover

Brushy, nasty, hard-to-walk areas. That's where they live.

If it sucks for you — perfect for them.

Burn (Burn Scar)

Area affected by wildfire. New growth = incredible food source. Bears especially LOVE these.

Dixie Fire zones = jackpot for CA bear hunting.

Orientation & Conditions

The invisible game

Aspect

Direction a slope faces. North-facing = cooler, wetter, thicker (bedding). South-facing = warmer, drier (feeding).

Thermals

Daily wind shifts. Morning: rising. Evening: falling.

This controls EVERYTHING about animal movement.

Micro Terrain

The subtle kill zones

Edge

Where two habitat types meet (timber to open, burn to forest). Animals feed here. Predators hunt here.

Pinch Point

Where terrain forces movement into a narrow path. Saddles, cliffs, steep walls. Ambush heaven.

Sidehill

Slope animals traverse instead of going straight up/down. Most common travel pattern.

Game Trail

Worn animal path. Connects bedding to feeding to water.

Bonus Terms

You'll hear these in the wild

Bluff / Cliff Band

Steep or vertical rock. Forces movement around — creates pinch points.

Chute

Steep narrow gully. Travel corridor or escape route.

Flat

Large level area (often on top or mid-slope). Feeding or staging area.

Glassing Knob

High point used to observe terrain.

Your throne. Your strategy center.
